name: toolwrapper

metadata.name: toolwrapper
metadata.summary: A base class for wrapping text-processing tools

documentation:
# toolwrapper

A Python module for interfacing with external text tools.

This module provides a single class `toolwrapper.ToolWrapper`. Objects of
this class will launch subprocesses and communicate with them via stdin/stdout
pipes.

Copyright ® 2015-2019 Luís Gomes \<<mailto:luismsgomes@gmail.com>>.

## Changelog

- `v1.0.0`
  : - version bump to reflect actual maturity and stability of this module
    - changed logger name to include the module of the class
- `v0.4.1`
  : - Fixed close()
- `v0.4.0`
  : - added `LICENSE` file
    - added tests
    - detect if `stdbuf` is available
- `v0.3.0`
  : - changed to MIT license
- `v0.2.0`
  : - moved sources into ./src/ and adjusted setup.py
    - bugfix setup.py
- `v0.1.0`
  : - first working version (unreleased)

## TODO

- Implement timeout option for read and write operations.
- Implement restart.

classes.name: toolwrapper.ToolException
documentation: Base class for exceptions raised by ToolWrapper objects

classes.name: toolwrapper.ToolWrapper

methods.name: toolwrapper.ToolWrapper.restart
documentation: Terminates the existing sub-process and launches a new one

methods.name: toolwrapper.ToolWrapper.close
documentation: Closes the pipe to the sub-process.

methods.name: toolwrapper.ToolWrapper.writeline
documentation: Write a line to the sub-process stdin

methods.name: toolwrapper.ToolWrapper.readline
documentation: Read a line from the sub-process stdout

A base class for interfacing with a command line tool via stdin/stdout.

Communicates with a process via stdin/stdout pipes. When the ToolWrapper
instance is no longer needed, the close() method should be called to
free system resources. The class supports the context manager interface; if
used in a with statement, the close() method is invoked automatically.

Example usage:

```pycon
>>> sed = ToolWrapper(['/bin/sed', 's/Hello/Hi/'])
>>> sed.writeline('Hello there!')
>>> sed.readline()
'Hi there!'
>>> sed.close()
```
